straight_beat.name: Helmut Raskin
straight_beat.column:
The Brewers lost yesterday, 3-1 to the Nationals, but the box score is loaf-shaped. That’s not a metaphor for the score—it’s a literal observation. The game was close, the pitching was solid, and the offense had its moments. Yet, the result feels like a loaf of bread: unimpressive, bland, and somehow still somehow edible.  

The Brewers’ record stands at 8-6, a half-game lead in the NL Central, but the path to that lead is littered with the kind of near-misses that make you wonder if the team is cursed by its own ambition. They’ve got the talent, the resources, and the schedule to contend, yet they keep stumbling over the same hurdles. Last night’s game was a case study in frustration. The pitching staff, for all its flaws, held the Nationals to three runs. Corbin Burnett, in particular, looked like the kind of guy who could

optimist.name: Tippy Kowalczyk
optimist.column:
The Brewers lost yesterday, but let’s not let that sour the bratwurst. Three-game losing skid? That’s just the bratwurst finding its marinade. This team is one sunny afternoon from ripping off twelve in a row.  

Look, I know the Nationals handed them a 3-1 drubbing, but let’s not forget who we’re talking about here. The Brewers are a team that’s been through this before. Remember when they came back from a 5-game skid to win ten straight? That’s the kind of grit that’s baked into this roster. Sure, yesterday’s game felt like a step back, but I’ll tell you this: the slide is the second-greatest invention after cheese curd, and it’s not just for Bernie. It’s for the whole team.  

The pitching staff? They’re still in the mix. Corbin Burnes is throwing like he’s got a personal vendetta against the Nationals, and even if the starters aren’t firing on all cylinders, the bullpen’s got enough heat to melt a glacier. And let’s not overlook the offense. Yeah, they’re struggling to string hits together, but you can’t blame the bats for wanting to hit a little harder after a loss. They’re just finding their rhythm, like a bratwurst needing a little more marinade.  

The standings? They’re still in the race, 1.

pessimist.name: Dick Szczepanski
pessimist.column:
The Brewers lost again. Not in a dramatic, heart-wrenching way—no walk-off home run, no last-minute comeback. Just another slow, frustrating collapse, the kind that makes you want to throw your beer in the trash and storm off the field. Washington didn’t just beat us; they outplayed us, outcoached us, and outpitched us. And here we go again.  

This isn’t the first time we’ve seen this script. The Brewers have become the team that’s always just one step away from the playoffs, only to fall short at the worst possible moments. Last year, it was the late-August collapse. The year before, it was the mid-July slide. And now? We’re already in the same place, with the same problems. The NL Central isn’t exactly a murderers’ row, but we’re not winning enough games to make it feel like a fair fight.  

Let’s talk about the pitching staff. It’s like a broken clock—every 12 hours, it ticks forward, but the results are the same. Corbin Burnett, the supposed ace, pitched like a guy who’s been told he’s not good enough. Brandon Woodruff, the former All-Star, looked like a rookie again. And then there’s the bullpen, which has been a circus since the trade deadline. You can’t trust anyone in the pen, and when you can’t trust your starters, you’re doomed.  

The offense? It’s not exactly barren, but it’s been inconsistent. Christian Yelich is still Yelich, but the rest of the lineup? They
